title: Chapter 3: Revenge, Served Expensive
content: The pho hits me before I'm through the door.

Star anise and fish sauce and that specific char of beef bones simmered for hours — the smell is a time machine. Six months of Prep Cook Chloe, showing up at six a.m., knuckles cracked from dish soap, hair tied back with a rubber band I found on the floor. The smell should make me nauseous. It should trigger some PTSD-lite montage of grease burns and withheld tips. Instead my brain just goes: *oh, pho,* with about the same emotional range as a weather report.
I'm wearing Balenciaga.

Let me say that again, because I'm still not used to it: I'm wearing Balenciaga. A structured leather jacket that cost more than three months of rent at my old place, and when I run my fingers along the collar, the leather is so soft it feels like touching a lie. Like touching something that shouldn't belong to a person who used to fold napkins for a living. This morning I walked into the SoHo boutique and the sales associate looked at me — the old me, the me with the posture of someone who expects to be asked to leave — and I watched her recalculate in real time when the card went through. Fifty thousand dollars in the account and I bought a jacket on impulse like I was grabbing a pack of gum at CVS. That's what money does. It turns every decision into a nothing decision.

The restaurant is Pho Viet Huong on Grand Street. Same cracked tile floor. Same fluorescent tube flickering in the back by the walk-in. Same menu board with the smudged "SPECIAL" written in red marker that hasn't been special since the Bush administration.
Same manager behind the counter.

Hung Tran. Fifty-four, built like a fire hydrant, voice like a fire alarm. The man who fired me over a broken plate — a single plate, a white plate with a blue rim that slipped off my forearm during a Friday rush. He screamed at me in front of the entire kitchen. Screamed like I'd burned the building down. Screamed like I'd personally insulted his ancestors. Then he docked my pay for the plate and told me not to come back.
He looks up when the door chimes.

Nothing. He sees a woman in a Balenciaga jacket and his brain files me under Not A Problem. Maybe a foodie, maybe a tourist, maybe someone who got lost looking for the trendy spot two doors down. He offers the tepid smile he reserves for people who might tip.
Then the recognition kicks in.

It's slow, like a modem connecting. The eyes narrow. The mouth shifts. I watch it happen in stages — confusion, double-take, and then that specific face people make when their brain is rewriting a story it thought it already finished. *That's the dish girl. The plate girl. The one I threw out.* But she's wearing a jacket that costs more than his car.

I walk to the counter. I don't hurry. I don't slow down. I move like someone who has nowhere to be but also every right to be here. The line cooks in the back have stopped moving. The server with the face tattoo — Danny, I think — freezes mid-bus with a tray of glasses.

I reach into my bag. Pull out the check I had the bank print this morning — not a transfer, not a Venmo, an actual paper check, because this is theater and theater requires props. I slap it on the counter. The sound is louder than I expected. Sharp. Final.
Five thousand dollars.

The number sits there in black ink. More than my entire back pay. More than the plate cost. More than the screaming cost, although you can't really put a price on being humiliated in front of twelve strangers. Actually, you can. Apparently it's five thousand.

"For the plate I broke," I say. My voice is calm. Cold. I'm reading a grocery list. I'm confirming a delivery time. I'm doing anything except having a feeling. "And the screaming. Mostly the screaming."

Hung picks up the check. His hands are shaking. Not a lot — he's not having a breakdown or anything — but enough that the paper trembles like a leaf. He stares at the number. His mouth opens. Nothing comes out.
I don't wait for the thank you. I don't wait for the apology. Those things would require him to be a different person than he is, and I already know who he is. I turn around and walk out.
On the sidewalk, I wait.
For the rush. For the *hell yes*. For the fist-pump, the victory lap, the montage music. I stand there in my Balenciaga jacket and I wait for it.
It doesn't come.

What comes instead is a small, tidy sense of correctness. Like checking off a box on a to-do list. *Revenge: manager. Done.* The satisfaction is structural — it has the shape of satisfaction, the architecture of it, but the rooms are empty. Nobody's home.
I get on the L train and head for art school.

The admin office at Parsons smells like toner and fake leather and the specific desperation of people filling out FAFSA forms. I used to sit in the plastic chairs by the window and stress about whether my meal plan would cover dinner. Now I sit in the same chairs and wait for the dean to finish processing my payment, which — and I can't stress this enough — I made on my phone while standing at the counter. Three fees. Storage, because they kept my portfolio locked up after the "dispute." Late, because I couldn't afford to claim it when the dispute was happening. And a donation, because apparently you can buy the moral high ground now and the price is whatever makes the dean's eyes go wide.
The dean's eyes go very wide.
"Miss Hart, we can't—" he starts.

"You can." I take the portfolio from his hands. It's my work. *My* work. Ten months of charcoal and ink and three a.m. sessions in the shared studio, the one with the bad lighting and the radiator that clanked like it was arguing with itself. I know every page. I know the smudge on the corner of the self-portrait where I rested my thumb. I know the crease on the charcoal study where my portfolio bag got caught in the subway door.
I know it better than Ashlyn Kerr ever did.

She's in the main hallway, because of course she is. Ashlyn Kerr: blonde, white, trust fund, last name on a building. She stole my portfolio the way rich people steal everything — confidently, with the assumption that the system would back her up. And it did. For ten months, it did. I filed a complaint and got a form email. I showed timestamped photos and got told "these things are complicated." I cried in the bathroom and a security guard asked if I was okay and I said yes because what else do you say?

Now she's holding court by the vending machines with three friends who all have the same highlighted hair and the same puffy vest. She's laughing about something. Probably someone. She's always laughing about someone.
I walk up. I don't announce myself. I don't clear my throat. I hold up the portfolio, open to the self-portrait — my face, my hands, my smudge in the corner — and I say:
"This is mine. You knew it was mine. And now everyone's going to know."

Ashlyn's face does a thing. It crumbles. That's the only word for it. The confident architecture of her expression just folds inward, like a building in a demolition video. The jaw drops. The eyebrows collapse. The calculated warmth in her eyes — the warmth she uses like a tool, like a credit card — just vacates. Her friends go silent. Not supportive-silence. Rewriting-the-narrative silence. The kind where they're already calculating distance.
It should taste like honey.
It tastes like tap water. Room temperature tap water from a fountain you don't even want to drink from but you do because you're thirsty and it's there.

Ashlyn says my name. It comes out like a question. Like she's never had to say it before and the syllables are unfamiliar on her tongue. I don't answer. I close the portfolio and walk away and my Balenciaga jacket does that cool leather thing where it catches the hallway light and I look like a person who just won.
I feel like a person who just ran an errand.
*Revenge: art thief. Done.*

Derek's apartment is on Grand Street in Williamsburg, which is itself a kind of revenge — he pays $3,200 for a studio with a murphy bed and a view of a dumpster, and he thinks this makes him a New Yorker. He thinks a lot of stupid things. He thought crypto was going to replace the dollar. He thought I wouldn't find out about the girl from his meetup. He thought "it meant nothing" was an apology.

I still have his spare key. He asked for it back once, over text, three weeks after I found out, and I left him on read because I was busy crying in a way that seemed more important than key logistics.
The lock turns easy.

His apartment smells like Axe body spray and the specific dude-musk of unwashed sheets, and I'm hit with a memory so specific it's almost physical — the way he used to pull me into his chest and I'd breathe in that same smell and think *this is fine, this is enough, this is what people do, they find someone whose smells they can tolerate and they call it love.* God, I was an idiot. But being an idiot at least felt like something.
He's asleep. Of course he is. It's 10 a.m. and Derek doesn't believe in working before noon because he's "on a crypto schedule," which is unemployed person with a VPN.

I pull out the Sharpie. Pop the cap. The chemical smell hits — that sharp, instant, almost alcoholic burn that goes straight from your nose to the part of your brain that says *permanent.* I write on the check: *for the therapy you need.* The Sharpie squeaks on the paper. Each letter is careful and deliberate because this message needs to be readable and also because slowing down is the only way I can feel anything and even then it's tenuous.

I set the check on his pillow. Right next to his face. He'll see it when he opens his eyes, which — there. His eyelids flicker. Confusion first. Then his brain processes the check, the handwriting, and then — I watch it happen — the woman standing in his doorway in a jacket that costs more than his annual rent.
Terrified. He's terrified.
Good. That's good. That's the appropriate response.

I'm already gone. Down the stairs, out the door, onto the street. The morning sun hits my face and the Balenciaga leather presses warm against my shoulders and I wait for it. The satisfaction. The catharsis. The moment where the music swells and I feel like the main character.
I wait.
*Revenge: ex-boyfriend. Done.*
The wait continues.
It doesn't come.

The coffee shop is on Lorimer. Not Dunkin'. Not the bodega counter where I used to get fifty-cent refills. A real coffee shop, the kind with reclaimed wood and a tip jar that says "tips make us frothy" and a menu that uses the word "single-origin" like it's a moral virtue. My latte costs seven dollars. I didn't even blink. Seven dollars used to be two days of lunch and now it's a beverage and I'm not sure which version of that sentence is sadder.

The latte tastes like — I don't know. It tastes like latte. Steamed milk, espresso, a hint of something the menu described as "notes of dark chocolate" but which my mouth is just processing as *brown.* My taste buds are phoning it in. My whole body is phoning it in.
I sit by the window. I stir. The spoon goes in circles, a small orbit of nothing.

Three enemies. Three checks. Three moments of absolute, crystalline power where I got to be the person on top instead of the person underneath. The manager with his shaking hands. Ashlyn with her crumbling face. Derek with his terror. I got everything I wanted. Every single thing I would have dreamed about two days ago — I would have *killed* for this. I would have sold what's left of my soul for a fraction of this satisfaction.
And the satisfaction is—
Not there.

The volume on my feelings is at 60%. Maybe 55%. I'm doing the math in my head like it's a battery indicator, which is itself a sign that something's wrong. You don't calculate your joy. You don't spreadsheet your catharsis. But here I am, running diagnostics on my own emotional output, and the numbers are not good.

I got revenge and it felt like errands. A to-do list. A circuit. I should be flying. I should be texting someone — anyone — about what I just did. I should be screaming into a pillow or dancing in my apartment or doing whatever people do when the universe finally, *finally* pays them back for every indignity they've swallowed.
I'm sitting. Stirring a $7 latte. Watching the foam make a tiny whirlpool that goes nowhere.

My phone buzzes. Follower notification. I glance at it: the number blurs past, something with a lot of zeroes, but I'm not really looking because my eyes have drifted to the coffee shop window and the reflection staring back at me.

The woman in the window is wearing a Balenciaga jacket. She has good posture — new posture, the kind that comes from not worrying about rent. Her hair is clean and her skin is clear because she bought skincare today, expensive skincare, the kind that comes in weighted glass bottles and smells like someone's idea of luxury. She looks like she has her life together. She looks like the kind of person who wins.
I don't recognize her.

I stare. She stares back. Somewhere between the restaurant and the art school and Derek's murphy bed, I became someone else, and the transition was so smooth I didn't even feel the seam. Two days ago I was Chloe Hart, dishwasher, art thief victim, cheated-on girlfriend. Now I'm Chloe Hart, viral sensation, walking checkbook, revenge expert. Same name. Different animal. And the distance between those two people is measured in something I can't name, something that feels less like growth and more like erosion.
Why isn't she happy?

The revenge worked. The money works. The fame works — five million people are watching me right now, apparently, though I don't know how I know that, the number just pulses at the edge of my vision like a heartbeat. Everything I wanted has arrived. The universe cut the check and I cashed it. So why am I sitting here feeling like I'm watching a movie of someone else's life?

And why — I look down at my phone — is there a notification I don't remember seeing? A pop-up, already dismissed, already gone, from an app I didn't install from a name I'm trying not to think about. It was there. I must have swiped it away without looking. An accident. A reflex.
I stir my latte. The coffee shop plays some indie song about feeling nothing. The universe has a sense of humor.
I'm not laughing.
